S30: Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. A B S T R A C T Background Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), also known as motor neuron disease, is a fatal neurodegenerative disease.
S31: Neuromuscular respiratory failure is the most common cause of death, which usually occurs within two to five years of the disease onset.
S32: Supporting respiratory function with mechanical ventilation may improve survival and quality of life.
S33: This is the second update of a review first published in 2009.
S34: Objectives To assess the eﬀects of mechanical ventilation (tracheostomy-assisted ventilation and non-invasive ventilation (NIV)) on survival, functional measures of disease progression, and quality of life in ALS, and to evaluate adverse events related to the intervention.
S35: Search methods We searched the Cochrane Neuromuscular Specialised Register, the Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL), MEDLINE, Embase, CINAHL Plus, and AMED on 30 January 2017.
S36: We also searched two clinical trials registries for ongoing studies.
S37: Selection criteria Randomised controlled trials (RCTs) and quasi-RCTs involving non-invasive or tracheostomy-assisted ventilation in participants with a clinical diagnosis of ALS, independent of the reported outcomes.
S38: We included comparisons with no intervention or the best standard care.
S39: Data collection and analysis For the original review, four review authors independently selected studies for assessment.
S40: Two review authors reviewed searches for this update.
S41: All review authors independently extracted data from the full text of selected studies and assessed the risk of bias in studies that met the inclusion criteria.
S42: We attempted to obtain missing data where possible.
S43: We planned to collect adverse event data from the included studies.
S44: Main results For the original Cochrane Review, the review authors identified two RCTs involving 54 participants with ALS receiving NIV.
S45: There were no new RCTs or quasi-RCTs at the first update.
S46: One new RCT was identified in the second update but was excluded for the reasons outlined below.

S51: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews Incomplete data were available for one published study comparing early and late initiation of NIV (13 participants).
S52: We contacted the trial authors, who were not able to provide the missing data.
S53: The conclusions of the review were therefore based on a single study of 41 participants comparing NIV with standard care.
S54: Lack of (or uncertain) blinding represented a risk of bias for participant- and clinician- assessed outcomes such as quality of life, but it was otherwise a well-conducted study with a low risk of bias.
S55: The study provided moderate-quality evidence that overall median survival was significantly diﬀerent between the group treated with NIV and the standard care group.
S56: The median survival in the NIV group was 48 days longer (219 days compared to 171 days for the standard care group (estimated 95% confidence interval 12 to 91 days, P = 0.0062)).
S57: This survival benefit was accompanied by an enhanced quality of life.
S58: On subgroup analysis, in the subgroup with normal to moderately impaired bulbar function (20 participants), median survival was 205 days longer (216 days in the NIV group versus 11 days in the standard care group, P = 0.0059), and quality of life measures were better than with standard care (low-quality evidence).
S59: In the participants with poor bulbar function (21 participants), NIV did not prolong survival or improve quality of life, although there was significant improvement in the mean symptoms domain of the Sleep Apnea Quality of Life Index by some measures.
S60: Neither trial reported clinical data on intervention-related adverse eﬀects.
S61: Authors' conclusions Moderate-quality evidence from a single RCT of NIV in 41 participants suggests that it significantly prolongs survival, and low-quality evidence indicates that it improves or maintains quality of life in people with ALS.
S62: Survival and quality of life were significantly improved in the subgroup of people with better bulbar function, but not in those with severe bulbar impairment.
S63: Adverse eﬀects related to NIV should be systematically reported, as at present there is little information on this subject.
S64: More RCT evidence to support the use of NIV in ALS will be diﬀicult to generate, as not oﬀering NIV to the control group is no longer ethically justifiable.
S65: Future studies should examine the benefits of early intervention with NIV and establish the most appropriate timing for initiating NIV in order to obtain its maximum benefit.
S66: The eﬀect of adding cough augmentation techniques to NIV also needs to be investigated in an RCT.
S67: Future studies should examine the health economics of NIV.
S68: Access to NIV remains restricted in many parts of the world, including Europe and North America.
S69: We need to understand the factors, personal and socioeconomic, that determine access to NIV.
S70: P L A I N L A N G U A G E S U M M A R Y Mechanical ventilation for people with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis/motor neuron disease Review question Does mechanical ventilation improve the survival of people with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS)?
S71: How does it aﬀect disease progression and quality of life, and does it have any unwanted eﬀects?
S72: Background Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis, also known as motor neuron disease, is a condition in which nerves that control movement are lost.
S73: Management of ALS has evolved rapidly in the last 10 years.
S74: Although there is still no cure, some treatments can help manage symptoms.
S75: Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis causes progressive muscle weakness, including weakness of the muscles used in breathing.
S76: Failure of ventilation (the capacity to move air in and out of the lungs) is an important cause of death in ALS.
S77: Mechanical ventilation is a method in which machines support the person's breathing.
S78: Mechanical ventilation may be invasive or non-invasive.
S79: Invasive ventilation involves insertion of a tube into the throat (tracheostomy).
S80: Non-invasive ventilation (NIV) is a method of helping people breathe that does not require a tracheostomy.
S81: Non-invasive ventilation supports breathing via a mask on the face or nose that is connected via tubing to a small portable ventilator.
S82: Study characteristics In this updated review, we examined the evidence from two randomised trials of NIV in ALS involving a total of 54 participants.
S83: One of the trials, which studied when to start NIV, provided no usable data.
S84: A third trial, identified in a clinical trials register, currently has no published results.
S85: Key results and quality of the evidence Complete data were only available from a single trial of 41 participants.
S86: The results of this trial provided moderate-quality evidence that NIV significantly prolongs survival, and low-quality evidence that it improves or maintains quality of life compared to standard care.
S87: Median survival was increased by an estimated 48 days, from 171 to 219 days.
S88: The survival benefit from NIV was much greater in people with ALS in whom the muscles used for speaking, chewing, and swallowing (bulbar muscles) were either unaﬀected or only moderately weak.
S89: Among these 20 participants, the median survival with NIV was increased by an estimated 205 days (216 days with NIV, compared to 11 days with standard care).
S90: Quality of life was also maintained in participants with mild to moderate bulbar weakness.
S91: In the 21 participants with severe bulbar weakness, NIV did not prolong survival or maintain quality of life scores, although a sleep-related symptoms score improved.
S92: Neither trial reported on adverse eﬀects.
S93: Participants and clinicians were aware of the treatment groups, which can influence quality of life assessments.

S154: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews B A C K G R O U N D Amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS), also known as motor neuron disease (MND), is a fatal neurodegenerative disease characterised by loss of upper and lower motor neurons in the brain and spinal cord (Brooks 1994; Brooks 2000).
S155: The incidence of ALS is 1 to 2 per 100,000 of the population, and the age-specific incidence and mortality rates peak at 55 to 75 years (Worms 2001).
S156: The average life expectancy is two to three years from the onset of symptoms, although 10% of people with ALS may survive for 10 years or more (Haverkamp 1995; Turner 2003).
S157: Death usually results from respiratory failure due to denervation weakness in respiratory muscles.
S158: Respiratory muscle function at any time point during the disease trajectory is the most important predictor of survival and an important predictor of quality of life (Bach 1995; Haverkamp 1995; Vitacca 1997; Stambler 1998; Fitting 1999; Chaudri 2000; Bourke 2001; Lyall 2001a; Varrato 2001; Lechtzin 2002).
S160: forced vital capacity and sniﬀ nasal inspiratory pressure) are useful in monitoring the progression of respiratory muscle weakness, but no single test of respiratory function can reliably predict the onset of respiratory failure.
S161: Furthermore, respiratory function tests have limitations in people with bulbar weakness, who cannot blow eﬀectively (Lyall 2001a).
S162: Assisted ventilation has long been used to support ventilation in respiratory failure (Annane 2014).
S163: Assisted ventilation can be provided by invasive (tracheostomy ventilation (TV)) and non-invasive (NIV) means.
S164: Tracheostomy ventilation can prolong survival for many years (Bach 1993; Cazzolli 1996), but it is resource-intensive and risks ventilator entrapment, which exacts a significant emotional toll on people with ALS and their carers (Moss 1993; Cazzolli 1996; Moss 1996).
S165: Tracheostomy ventilation may prolong life in the face of increasing disability and dependency, and hence quality of life may not be sustained.
S166: Nevertheless, people aﬀected by ALS are increasingly aware of this option.
S167: Tracheostomy ventilation in ALS is not encouraged in Europe and North America (Hayashi 1997; Borasio 1998; Yamaguchi 2001).
S168: In Japan, however, TV is the predominant form of ventilation oﬀered to people with ALS, and the cost is fully covered by the government and medical insurance (Kawata 2008).
S169: Non-invasive ventilation is another option for treating respiratory failure in people with ALS.
S170: Non-invasive ventilation utilises a face or nasal mask and a volume-cycled or bilevel pressure-limited ventilator to provide an intermittent positive pressure to support ventilation.
S171: Until the turn of the century, the use of NIV varied greatly across North America and Europe (Melo 1999; Borasio 2001; Bradley 2001; Cedarbaum 2001; Chio 2001; Bourke 2002).
S172: Evidence from several retrospective and some prospective studies indicated that NIV may be associated with gain in survival (Pinto 1995; Aboussouan 1997; Kleopa 1999; Bach 2002), improved quality of life (Hein 1997; Hein 1999; Aboussouan 2001; Bourke 2001; Jackson 2001; Lyall 2001b), and improved cognitive function (Newsom- Davis 2001).
S173: People with ALS who have little or no bulbar muscle weakness may tolerate NIV better than those with significant bulbar involvement (Cazzolli 1996; Aboussouan 1997).
S174: In the absence of a randomised controlled trial (RCT), uncertainties remained over the benefits and unwanted eﬀects of TV and NIV.
S175: Annane 2014, a Cochrane Review of nocturnal mechanical ventilation for a mixed group of people with chronic hypoventilation, identified eight randomised trials, three of which involved people with ALS.
S176: This review concluded that nocturnal ventilation may relieve chronic hypoventilation-related symptoms and prolong survival, but that the quality of the studies was poor and the benefit of long-term mechanical ventilation should be confirmed in further trials.
S177: The evidence was thought to be strongest for NIV in people with ALS.
S178: Over the past few years, the use of NIV in ALS has greatly increased (O'Neill 2012).
S179: An RCT evaluated the eﬀects of NIV on survival and quality of life in people with ALS (Bourke 2006).
S180: The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence UK (NICE) has published guidelines on the use of NIV in people with ALS (NICE 2010).
S181: The aim of this review was to assimilate the evidence for mechanical ventilation in ALS and to assess the benefits and unwanted eﬀects of TV and NIV.
S182: The conclusions of Radunovic 2009, the original version of this review, were based on the results of this single study of 41 participants (Bourke 2006), as the other available study provided no usable data (Jackson 2001).
S183: For this 2017 update, we identified no new RCTs or quasi-RCTs.
S184: O B J E C T I V E S To assess the eﬀects of mechanical ventilation (tracheostomy- assisted ventilation and non-invasive ventilation) on survival, functional measures of disease progression, and quality of life in ALS, and to evaluate adverse events related to the intervention.
S185: M E T H O D S Criteria for considering studies for this review Types of studies All RCTs and quasi-RCTs involving NIV or TV.
S186: Quasi-RCTs are trials where treatment allocation was intended to be random but may have been biased (e.g.
S187: alternate allocation or allocation according to the day of the week).
S188: We selected studies independently of reported outcomes.
S189: Types of participants People with a clinical diagnosis of ALS/MND (pure mixed upper motor neuron and lower motor neuron degeneration with supportive electromyogram) according to the El Escorial criteria (Brooks 1994; Brooks 2000), at any stage of disease and with any clinical pattern of the condition (e.g.
S191: Subgroups of interest were participants with or without significant bulbar symptoms as categorised by the authors of the papers reviewed.
S192: We did not consider trials of mixed neuromuscular or chest wall conditions, which are included in a separate review (Annane 2014).
S193: Types of interventions All forms of NIV (irrespective of pressure settings and timings) and TV, compared to no intervention or the best standard care.
S194: Types of outcome measures Our outcomes were not selection criteria, but rather a list of outcomes of interest within included studies.

S199: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews Primary outcomes The primary outcome was overall survival afer initiation of assisted ventilation, as assessed by a pooled hazard ratio using life table/Cox regression methods to combine disparate periods of observation from all studies.
S200: This would have been supplemented where possible by pooled estimates of the 75%, 50% (median) survival times and confidence intervals (CIs).
S201: This is to allow for the situation where the proportional hazards assumption, necessary for Cox regression, has not been met.
S203: Survival at one month and six months or longer.
S205: Quality of life assessed using validated health status questionnaires, e.g.
S206: 36-Item Short Form Health Survey (SF-36) at one month and six months or longer (Lyall 2001b).
S208: Any validated functional rating scale, such as the Amyotrophic Lateral Sclerosis Functional Rating Scale (ALSFRS) or the ALSFRS-Revised (ALSFRS 1996; Cedarbaum 1999), Norris (Norris 1974), or Appel scales at one month and six months or longer (Haverkamp 1995).
S210: The proportion of people experiencing adverse events related to mechanical ventilation.
S211: We would have considered adverse events in two categories.
S212: The first category would have included the proportion of participants experiencing any adverse event attributed to ventilation (e.g.
S213: fistulae, pneumothorax, bleeding, local infection, hospitalisation, or death), and the second category would have included participants experiencing severe complications of mechanical ventilation, including life- threatening episodes, prolonged hospitalisation, and death.
S214: Search methods for identification of studies We searched the following databases: • the Cochrane Neuromuscular Specialised Register (30 January 2017) (Appendix 1); • the Cochrane Central Register of Controlled Trials (CENTRAL) (30 January 2017, in the Cochrane Register of Studies Online (CRSO)) (Appendix 2); • MEDLINE (1966 to 30 January 2017) (Appendix 3); • Embase (1980 to 30 January 2017) (Appendix 4); • CINAHL Plus (1937 to 30 January 2017) (Appendix 5); • AMED (Allied and Complementary Medicine Database) (1985 to 30 January 2017) (Appendix 6).
S215: We also searched for ongoing or unpublished trials on 1 February 2017 in: • the US National Institutes of Health trials registry ClinicalTrials.gov (www.clinicaltrials.gov) (Appendix 7); • the World Health Organization International Clinical Trials Registry Platform (WHO ICTRP) (apps.who.int/trialsearch/) (Appendix 8).
S216: Data collection and analysis Selection of studies For the original review, all four review authors (AR, DA, KJ, NM) checked titles and abstracts identified by the searches for RCTs or quasi-RCTs, and two review authors (MKR and DA) reviewed the searches for the update.
S217: We obtained the full texts of all potentially relevant studies, which the review authors independently assessed.
S218: Data extraction and management All review authors independently extracted data onto a specially designed form.
S219: We tried to obtain missing or additional data from the study authors wherever possible.
S220: For our primary outcome, overall survival afer initiation of assisted ventilation, we planned to extract hazard ratios with standard errors or confidence intervals (CIs), or median survival times with 95% CIs, or the numbers required to construct a life table, for example the numbers surviving or failing to survive afer initiation of assisted ventilation for a sequence of specified time intervals.
S221: In any studies requiring translation, the translator would extract data directly onto a data extraction form.
S222: If possible, the review authors would have checked any numerical data extraction against the study report.
S223: Assessment of risk of bias in included studies All review authors decided which trials met the inclusion criteria for the review and assessed the risk of bias for the included studies.
S224: Any disagreements about inclusion were resolved by discussion between review authors.
S225: We completed a 'Risk of bias' assessment for all included studies according to the recommendations in the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions (Higgins 2011).
S226: We evaluated random sequence generation, allocation concealment, blinding (participants and personnel, and outcome assessors), incomplete outcome data, selective outcome reporting, and other sources of bias.
S227: We then made a judgement on each of these criteria of 'high risk', 'low risk', or 'unclear risk', where 'unclear risk' indicates an unclear or unknown risk of bias.
S228: Measures of treatment eﬀect In Appendix 9 we have reported in full methods that the review authors will follow if more trials become available in the future and meta-analysis becomes possible.

S260: Included studies All review authors agreed on the inclusion of two studies (Jackson 2001; Bourke 2006).
S261: The first study was a prospective randomised three-month study in three ALS centres in the USA (Jackson 2001).
S262: The study included 20 people with ALS (no age or sex provided), of whom 13 were randomised when overnight oximetry studies documented oxygen saturation below 90% for at least one cumulative minute throughout the duration of the study (a minimum of six hours) and the individual had at least two significant symptoms of nocturnal hypoventilation.
S263: There were two groups: an early group where the participants were started immediately on NIV (seven participants) and a late group (six participants) in whom NIV was initiated when forced vital capacity (FVC) was less than 50% predicted.
S264: The report provided no demographic characteristics for the participants.

S328: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews Other potential sources of bias In Jackson 2001, trial authors did not define nocturnal hypoventilation according to the universally accepted criteria; they accepted oxygen desaturation below 90% for at least one cumulative minute as evidence of nocturnal hypoventilation.
S329: Nocturnal oximetry showing oxygen desaturation below 88% for at least five consecutive minutes or oxygen saturation below 90% for more than 5% of sleep time are considered suﬀicient to initiate NIV (Mehta 2001).
S330: Eﬀects of interventions See: Summary of findings for the main comparison Non-invasive ventilation compared with standard care for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS) Both included studies used NIV.
S331: Jackson 2001 provided insuﬀicient data to be included in meta- analyses, and this prevented pooled analysis of either primary or secondary outcome measures.
S332: Our review was therefore based on the results of one study, Bourke 2006.
S333: Neither were we able to obtain individual participant data or life tables from the Bourke 2006 trialists.
S334: Primary outcome Bourke 2006 showed that the overall median survival afer initiation of assisted ventilation was significantly diﬀerent between the NIV and standard care groups (P = 0.0062).
S335: The median survival for the NIV group participants was 48 days longer than the standard care group participants (219 days compared to 171 days).
S336: The published information did not provide a 95% confidence interval (CI) for the median survival diﬀerence (48 days) or for our secondary outcomes.
S337: We were able to derive approximate CIs from P values and median survival estimates under an assumption that median survival follows a lognormal distribution.
S338: A statistical method based on a lognormal survival model gave the following estimates for the 95% CI: 12 to 91 days for the estimated 48-day survival diﬀerence (private communication from statistical referee Dan Moore).
S339: The overall median survival of the subgroup with better (good or moderately impaired) bulbar function was also significantly diﬀerent in the NIV group (P = 0.0059), with NIV group participants surviving 205 days longer than the standard care group participants (median 216 days in NIV group versus 11 days in the standard care group).
S340: In participants with poor bulbar function, NIV did not confer survival advantage (P = 0.92), with an overall median survival for NIV group participants of 39 days less than the standard care group (222 versus 261 days).
S341: We considered the quality of the survival evidence as moderate, as it was based on a single RCT (see Summary of findings for the main comparison).

S347: Bourke 2006 reported the time SAQLI and SF-36 scores were maintained above 75% of pre-randomisation assessment, and time-weighted mean improvements in SF-36 and sleep-related SAQLI (the median (range) values of AUC (area under the curve) above baseline divided by time from randomisation to death).
S348: We have reported here the summary scores but also provide the SAQLI symptoms score, which the Bourke 2006 trialists designated as primary.
S349: Summary scores We were unable to calculate mean diﬀerence and 95% CI from the available data.
S350: Based on interpretation of P values from two-sided tests of significance (0.05 significance level) on intention-to-treat data, the median time afer initiation of assisted ventilation that quality of life was maintained above 75% of the baseline, based on the SF-36 mental component summary (MCS), Physical Component Summary (PCS), and the SAQLI score, was significantly longer with NIV than standard care in the group as a whole and in the subgroup with normal and moderately impaired bulbar function.
S351: In participants with poor bulbar function, NIV conferred no significant benefit using these measures.

S354: The time-weighted mean improvement in the SF-36 PCS was greater in the participants with better bulbar function, but not in the whole group or in the subgroup with poor bulbar function.
S356: Other primary quality of life outcomes in the included study Bourke 2006 chose the SF-36 MCS (reported above) and the sleep- related SAQLI symptoms domain as primary quality of life outcome measures, based on the results of a pilot study.
S357: The study report and the trial protocol do not indicate whether these outcomes were prespecified as primary.
S358: The study showed that the median time the SAQLI symptoms domain score was maintained above 75% of the baseline afer initiation of NIV was significantly longer with NIV than with standard care in the group as a whole and in the subgroup with normal and moderately impaired bulbar function.
S359: In participants with poor bulbar function, NIV conferred no significant benefit in maintaining the SAQLI symptoms score above 75% of baseline.
S361: A significant diﬀerence in time-weighted mean improvement of SAQLI between the NIV and standard care group was present in the group as a whole and in both subgroups.

S375: D I S C U S S I O N Two reports of RCTs of nocturnal mechanical ventilation in ALS were available for this review.
S377: Since only one of the reports provided data suitable for analysis (Bourke 2006), we were not able to perform a meta-analysis.
S378: Bourke 2006 was designed to assess the eﬀect of NIV on survival and quality of life in people with ALS.
S379: The study provided moderate- quality evidence that NIV prolongs median survival, and low- quality evidence that it maintains quality of life in people with ALS overall.
S380: The benefit of NIV was striking in the subgroup of participants with better (normal or moderately impaired) bulbar function, but it is, however, important to note that six of nine participants in the standard group died within two weeks of randomisation, thus probably overestimating the eﬀect of NIV in this subgroup of people with ALS.
S381: Non-invasive ventilation did not prolong survival in people with ALS who had severe bulbar dysfunction, but did improve sleep-related symptoms in this subgroup.
S382: Despite the above shortcomings, Bourke 2006 has shown that NIV significantly improves quality of life over standard care, prolonging life for longer than is reported with riluzole (Miller 2012).
S383: The study has confirmed previous observations from non-randomised trials of survival advantage and improved quality of life in people with ALS who start and can tolerate NIV at the onset of respiratory impairment (Bach 1993; Pinto 1995; Aboussouan 1997; Kleopa 1999; Lyall 2001b).
S384: It is unlikely that there will be further RCTs of NIV in unselected cohorts of people with ALS.
S385: In the view of the review authors, evidence from Bourke 2006 supports the belief that NIV is a major advance in the management of ALS, and it would be unethical in future trials not to oﬀer NIV to people with ALS who have symptoms of nocturnal hypoventilation.
S386: The evidence supports benefit from NIV in people with ALS.
S387: The National Institute for Health and Care Excellence (NICE) carried out a cost-eﬀectiveness analysis using the Markov model and concluded that the use of NIV in the management of people with ALS represents a cost-eﬀective use of resources (NICE 2010).
S388: A UK-wide survey demonstrated that the provision of NIV to people with ALS is increasing (O'Neill 2012).
S389: A study has also reported on the obstacles people with ALS experience in using NIV and how to address them (Baxter 2013a).
S390: Some people with ALS are unable to tolerate NIV and decide to discontinue its use.
S391: Careful attention to secretion management, humidification, and comfortable interfaces may improve compliance with NIV.
S392: Detailed explanation to the patient along with emphasis of benefits may pre-empt diﬀiculties.
S393: A qualitative study has addressed concerns about the use of NIV in the terminal phase of the disease (Baxter 2013b).
S394: This study did not find the use of NIV in the last days of life to be associated with any adverse eﬀects.
S395: The authors advised rapid withdrawal rather than gradual weaning when patients express a wish to discontinue NIV.
S396: The study concluded that patients' wishes should guide the use of NIV at the end of life.
S397: Another study investigated the impact of NIV on the family carer and concluded that NIV had no significant impact on carer burden (Baxter 2013c).
S398: Conclusions from Bourke 2006 cannot be extended to people with ALS who do not have respiratory symptoms.
S399: It has been suggested that early treatment with NIV may oﬀer a survival benefit above that demonstrated in Bourke 2006.
S400: There is some evidence from non-controlled studies that early NIV improves survival and reduces decline of FVC in ALS (Carratu 2009).
S401: NCT01641965 is an ongoing RCT evaluating the impact of early NIV in people with ALS who have mild respiratory involvement.
S402: Jacobs 2016 is a pilot study that aimed to determine the feasibility of conducting a randomised, double-blind, placebo-controlled trial of early NIV versus sham NIV in people with ALS.
S403: The study was not powered to assess clinical outcomes.
S404: Non-invasive ventilation settings may require titration with disease progression.
S405: A randomised trial is evaluating the use of polysomnography in guiding the initiation and further titration of NIV therapy during the course of the disease (NCT01363882).
S406: Diﬀerent ventilator modes and settings are to be assessed in two NIV trials in people with ALS.
S407: The Italian multicentre randomised NIV study was designed to evaluate clinical eﬀicacy, the participants' tolerance and quality of life and the frequency of changing settings in people with ALS who are undergoing NIV with pressure support ventilation or volume-assisted ventilation (NCT00560287).
S408: However, this trial is compromised due to incomplete outcome data.
S409: The Columbia University study is designed to measure diﬀerence in pulmonary function and respiratory muscle pressure testing, gas exchange, and subjective dyspnoea between baseline and two diﬀerent ventilator modes (high- and low-level non-invasive positive pressure ventilation) (NCT00537446).
S410: Another study is evaluating the use of an Intelligent Volume-Assured Pressure Support (iVAPS) ventilator in ALS (NCT01746381).
S411: Most of these studies are not eligible for this review, which focuses on NIV compared to no NIV or standard care.
S412: They may, however, inform the best pressure settings of NIV and the ideal time of its initiation to obtain the maximum benefit.
S413: Another Cochrane Review of nocturnal ventilation for daytime hypoventilation in neuromuscular and chest wall disorders concluded, while noting several methodological limitations, that mechanical ventilation is associated with survival benefit in MND (Annane 2014).
S414: This conclusion was based on three trials (Pinto 1995; Jackson 2001; Bourke 2006).
S415: Jackson 2001 is included in this review, but we consider it to be at high risk of bias due to selective reporting.
S416: We excluded Pinto 1995 as we considered it a non- randomised controlled study.
S417: Another review on the management of respiratory problems in neurodegenerative disease concluded that the evidence to support the role of NIV was strongest for MND, although the evidence itself was weak (Jones 2012).
S418: A limitation of the evidence in this review is that it was based on a single RCT with 41 participants, and hence we assessed the quality of the evidence as moderate for improvement in survival.
S419: However, findings of this trial are consistent with the findings of several other studies, which together oﬀer strong support for the benefit of NIV for people with ALS in respiratory failure.

S425: We did not identify any study that addressed the adverse eﬀects of NIV.
S426: The review protocol did not define the quality of life outcome in detail.
S427: The protocol and study report of Bourke 2006 do not state whether the primary quality of life domains or analyses were prespecified (Bourke 2006; ISRCTN76330611).
S428: To reduce the risk of selective reporting in the review, we reported summary scores for both scales and both types of analysis, including results for the SAQLI symptoms score, which was a primary quality of life outcome in the trial.
S429: The review authors clarified eligible comparisons in the methods, explicitly excluding trials assessing the timing of initiation and other aspects of NIV delivery or specification.
S430: This decision excludes some studies of NIV, limiting its scope to the central question of the eﬀects of NIV versus 'standard care' or versus no NIV as originally intended.
S431: New, ongoing studies are addressing further issues about the timing of NIV initiation and further titration with the disease progression.
S432: Future reviews will assess diﬀerent modes, schedules, and initiation of ventilation.
S433: A U T H O R S ' C O N C L U S I O N S Implications for practice Moderate-quality evidence from a single randomised controlled trial (RCT) of non-invasive ventilation (NIV) involving 41 participants suggests that NIV significantly prolongs survival in people with amyotrophic lateral sclerosis (ALS).
S434: Low-quality evidence suggests that it maintains quality of life in people with ALS.
S435: Survival and some measures of quality of life were significantly improved in the subgroup of people with better bulbar function, but not in those with severe bulbar impairment.
S436: Adverse eﬀects related to NIV should be systematically reported, as at present there is little information on this subject.
S437: Implications for research More evidence in the form of RCTs to support the use of NIV in ALS will be diﬀicult to generate, as not oﬀering NIV to the control group is no longer ethically justifiable.
S438: Future studies should examine the benefits of early intervention with NIV and establish the most appropriate timing for initiating NIV, in order to obtain its maximum benefit.
S439: The eﬀect of adding cough augmentation techniques to NIV also needs to be ascertained in an RCT.
S440: Future studies should examine the health economics of NIV and factors influencing access to NIV.
S441: Access to NIV remains restricted in many parts of the world, including Europe and North America.
S442: We need to understand the factors, personal and socioeconomic, that determine access to NIV.

S905: Additional methods If meta-analysis becomes possible with the inclusion of more trials, we will use the following methods, which are based on a standard template developed by Cochrane Airways and modified by the Cochrane Neuromuscular Disease Group.
S906: Measures of treatment eﬀect For the primary outcome measure we planned to calculate an overall measure of treatment eﬀicacy combining survival results at diﬀerent time points.
S907: This measure is based on estimating a pooled hazard ratio (i.e.
S908: at any given time point the risk of death for the survivors in the treated group divided by risk of death for the survivors in the control group) as described by Parmar 1998.
S909: We wanted to use this measure rather than the summary risk ratio (RR) calculated by the Cochrane statistical sofware, Review Manager 5 (RevMan 2014), because the Parmar method uses all the data on survival from the whole observation period, whereas Review Manager 5 needs the survival rates at the same fixed point in time since the start of observation to be reported for all the studies if they are to be combined.
S910: For the secondary outcome measures reported as continuous variables, we planned to calculate a mean of the diﬀerence between their eﬀects using Review Manager 5.
S911: Had we found trials using dichotomous outcome measures such as death rates afer a fixed time, for example three months, we planned to obtain RR with 95% confidence interval (CI).
S912: Had we found trials where the studies measured continuous outcomes that are conceptually the same but are measured in diﬀerent ways (such as diﬀerent assessment scales), we planned to combine the results and express them as standardised mean diﬀerences (using standard deviation units) with 95% CI.
S913: Assessment of heterogeneity We planned to use the I2 statistic for quantifying inconsistency across trials in any meta-analysis, as described in Chapter 9 of the Cochrane Handbook for Systematic Reviews of Interventions, using the following as a rough guide for interpretation (Higgins 2011): • 0% to 40%: low heterogeneity; • 30% to 60%: may represent moderate heterogeneity; Mechanical ventilation for amyotrophic lateral sclerosis/motor neuron disease (Review) Copyright © 2018 The Cochrane Collaboration.
S914: Published by John Wiley & Sons, Ltd. 30 Cochrane Library Trusted evidence.
S917: Cochrane Database of Systematic Reviews • 50% to 90%: may represent substantial heterogeneity; • 75% to 100%: considerable heterogeneity.
S918: Assessment of reporting biases If in the future we identify more trials, we will assess small-study eﬀects using funnel plots; however, these must be interpreted with caution and are not considered useful if fewer than 10 trials are included in an analysis (Sterne 2011).
S919: Data synthesis We planned to use Review Manager 5 for data synthesis if possible.
S920: We planned to use a fixed-eﬀect analysis, and to use a random-eﬀects analysis in the event of unexplained heterogeneity.
S921: Subgroup analysis and investigation of heterogeneity Had we found heterogeneity of a moderate or greater level, we planned to first check the data and then consider whether pooling the data was justified.
S922: We would then have undertaken a sensitivity analysis by repeating the calculation omitting the trials at high risk of bias.
S923: If heterogeneity was not explained by variations in trial quality, we would have used a random-eﬀects approach to obtain the pooled estimates from the group of trials.
S924: Subgroups of interest were participants with or without significant bulbar symptoms as categorised by the authors of the papers reviewed.
S925: We considered non-randomised evidence concerning adverse events and the cost and cost-eﬀectiveness of diﬀerent forms of mechanical ventilation in the Discussion.
S926: Sensitivity analysis We planned to undertake sensitivity analyses to demonstrate the eﬀect of downweighting or ignoring those studies that were at high risk of bias on individual criteria, if suﬀicient numbers of trials and relevant data were available for meta-analysis.
